title: Building a Business Case for Falls Prevention Technology
description: Framework for justifying technology investment in falls prevention. ROI models, cost-of-inaction analysis, and board presentation strategy.

blocks.question: How do we justify investment in falls prevention technology to senior leadership?
blocks.directAnswer: Build a financial model with three components: cost of inaction (falls-related costs you're already paying), cost avoidance (costs prevented by intervention), and revenue upside (quality bonuses and referrer retention). Evidence-based technology interventions show 144% average ROI. Quantify risks using your actual claims data.

tldrBullets.text: Average falls hospitalization costs $6,000-$15,000. Preventable falls detection costs $5,000-$8,000 per incident avoided.
Cochrane evidence shows systematic screening and intervention reduce falls 20-30%, producing 3-5x ROI within 18 months.
HHVBP and value-based care programs reward quality outcomes with 2-5% bonus revenue on attributed volume.

blocks.expandedAnswer:
Leadership thinks in financial terms. The question isn't 'Is falls prevention important?' It's 'Does the benefit justify the cost?' Your job is to build a rigorous financial case using your organization's actual data.

Start with cost of inaction. How many falls does your organization experience annually? What's the average cost per fall (hospitalization, readmission, staff time, litigation risk)? If you manage 500 home care cases with 2% annual fall rate, that's 10 falls per year at $10,000 average cost, equalling $100,000 annual cost.

Next, estimate cost avoidance. Systematic screening and intervention reduce falls 20-30%. If you prevent 3 falls annually, that's $30,000 cost avoidance. Add caregiver retention benefit (reduced turnover saves $8,000-$12,000 per prevented departure) and case retention (lost case costs $140,000 profit, preventing one loss pays for intervention).

Technology ROI typically emerges within 12-18 months. Cochrane reviews estimate evidence-based interventions achieve up to 144% average ROI. Use conservative estimates in your model (20% falls reduction, not 30%). Let the board see that even conservative assumptions justify investment.

sources.title: Cochrane: Interventions for Preventing Falls in Older People in Care Facilities
Journal of the American Geriatrics Society: Cost-Effectiveness of Falls Interventions
Home Care Association UK: Technology ROI Benchmarking

blocks.body:
Why you need a business case: The cost of inaction

Falls are expensive. Hospital admission averages $6,000-$15,000. Readmission within 30 days adds another $4,000-$8,000. Long-term care placement following falls costs $25,000-$40,000 annually. Lost revenue from case discharge compounds these costs.

But most organizations don't add these costs up. Finance and clinical teams work separately. Claims are paid but not tracked back to individual cases or organizational patterns. Building a business case requires pulling data together: claims cost, case loss, caregiver turnover, and operational disruption from falls-related crises.

Litigation risk is harder to quantify but real. A family suing after a preventable fall might claim $50,000-$200,000 depending on injury and negligence standard. Professional liability insurance costs more when claims history is poor. Reputational damage from poor safety records reduces referrals and damages brand.

Leadership needs to see the full picture. If falls cost your organization $150,000 annually but no business case links these costs to intervention investment, the budget stays allocated to reactive response rather than prevention.

The cost-of-inaction model: Quantifying falls impact

Create a spreadsheet with your actual data. Column 1: incident category (hospitalization, readmission, case loss, caregiver turnover). Column 2: annual volume (use claims data or care incident reports). Column 3: average cost per incident. Column 4: annual total cost.

Example for a 500-case home care operation: 10 hospitalizations × $10,000 = $100,000. 5 readmissions × $6,000 = $30,000. 2 case losses × $140,000 margin = $280,000. 8 caregiver departures due to fall-related stress × $10,000 = $80,000. Total annual cost of falls: $490,000.

This number is your anchor. Everything below it is an improvement. If falls prevention reduces falls by 20-30%, cost avoidance ranges $98,000-$147,000 annually. This is the financial benefit you're chasing.

Be conservative. Use 20% reduction, not 30%. Use published benchmarks for hospitalization cost, not optimistic estimates. Leadership will respect rigorous analysis more than over-optimistic projections.

Building the ROI case: Investment vs return

Systematize the math. If your falls prevention technology costs $100,000 to implement and $30,000 annually to operate, total year-one investment is $130,000. If you prevent 30% of falls, cost avoidance is $147,000 year one, producing $17,000 net benefit plus positive ROI trajectory.

Year two analysis is stronger: no implementation cost, only $30,000 operations. Cost avoidance $147,000. Net benefit $117,000. This demonstrates that initial investment is recovered within 12 months, with sustained benefit thereafter.

Add revenue upside separately. If your organization qualifies for HHVBP bonuses for falls reduction, this is additional revenue beyond cost avoidance. Quantify: what's your attributed patient volume, bonus rate, and falls reduction credit? If 5,000 attributed patients and 3% bonus rate, that's $150,000 incremental revenue per year if you reduce falls sufficiently.

Total financial case: $17,000 net benefit year one (conservative cost avoidance) plus $150,000 HHVBP bonus plus intangible benefits (brand reputation, staff satisfaction, reduced litigation risk). Leadership will approve this case.

Presenting your business case to the board

Structure your presentation around three sections: problem, solution, outcome. Problem: we're spending $490,000 annually on falls-related costs. Solution: systematic screening and intervention technology costs $130,000 year one. Outcome: we prevent 20-30% of falls, save $98,000-$147,000, and unlock HHVBP bonuses.

Use visuals. A simple chart showing current falls cost vs. projected cost after intervention makes the case quickly. A timeline showing 12-month ROI breakeven is powerful. Anticipate questions: caregiver adoption, clinical validation, staff training, compliance risk.

Emphasize that evidence backs the approach. Cochrane reviews document 20-30% falls reduction from systematic screening and intervention. This isn't new or unproven. You're applying established evidence to your population.

Address implementation risk. Outline rollout timeline (4-8 weeks for full deployment), staff training plan (1-2 days per team), and quality assurance process (monthly outcome review, quarterly clinical governance). Leadership respects teams that think about execution.

Close with the ask. 'I'm requesting approval to pilot falls prevention technology across 50 cases, measure outcomes for 12 weeks, and then expand to full portfolio if results match our financial projections. The pilot costs $15,000 and helps us validate assumptions before larger investment.'

Benchmarking and board metrics

Frame your case within industry context. Falls rates, hospitalization costs, and caregiver turnover vary, but benchmarks exist. Your organization's falls rate should fall within 5-8% of benchmark range. If you're above benchmark, the board will see opportunity to improve profitability through better outcomes.

Once technology is implemented, establish KPIs: monthly falls rate, prevention success rate, cost per prevented fall, and HHVBP qualification status. Report these to the board quarterly. Track actual ROI against projections. If you said $147,000 annual cost avoidance and deliver $160,000, the board recognizes value.

Competitive positioning also matters. If competitors have deployed falls prevention technology and you haven't, this is a referrer retention risk. Emphasize that technology is becoming standard of care. Early adoption positions your organization as quality leader.

stats.stat: Systematic falls screening and intervention reduce falls by 20-30% on average
stats.source: Cochrane Collaboration: Interventions for Preventing Falls in Older People in Care Facilities

stats.stat: Evidence-based falls interventions achieve up to 144% average ROI
stats.source: Journal of the American Geriatrics Society

stats.stat: Falls hospitalization costs $6,000-$15,000. Preventive technology pays for itself within 12-18 months
stats.source: UK Health Economics and Home Care Association

items.question: What if our falls rate is low? Do we still need falls prevention investment?
items.answer: Even low falls rates justify investment. If 2% of clients fall (1 client out of 50), and one fall costs $12,000, that's $12,000 in risk per 50 clients. Prevention technology preventing one fall per 50 clients over three years pays for itself. Plus, referrers expect evidence of systematic safety management.

items.question: How do we estimate cost avoidance if we don't have historical claims data?
items.answer: Use published benchmarks. Home care falls occur in 5-8% of population annually. Average hospitalization cost is $6,000-$15,000. Multiply your client volume by benchmark rate and cost. This gives a conservative estimate. Actual performance may vary, but the model is defensible.

items.question: What if the board wants a pilot before full investment?
items.answer: Pilots are reasonable. Propose a 12-week pilot with 50-100 high-risk clients. Measure falls prevention, cost avoidance, staff adoption, and client satisfaction. Report results quarterly. If outcomes meet projections, expand to full portfolio. Pilots de-risk board decision.

items.question: How long until we see ROI from falls prevention technology?
items.answer: Conservative models show 12-18 month payback. Cost avoidance from falls prevention begins immediately (prevented falls save cost month one). Implementation cost is front-loaded. Month 12 and beyond show strong positive ROI as prevention benefit accumulates.

items.question: Should we include staff retention benefit in our ROI model?
items.answer: Yes, but conservatively. Falls cause caregiver burnout and turnover. If technology prevents burnout-related departures, the benefit is real. Conservatively estimate $8,000 per prevented departure (recruitment, training, lost productivity). Only count departures directly attributable to falls.
